selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content: You must return JSON only. No markdown, no code fences, no commentary. Return a single JSON object with keys: mode, topic, opinion, reasoning_summary.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":37,"biological_sex":"male","gender":"transgender","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"retail worker","city_of_living":"Kansas City","employment_status":"self-employed","education_level":"lower secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"no formal education","parent_2":"bachelor's degree"},"marital_status":"married","children":1,"migration_status":"native American","psychological_feature":"anxious","religious_beliefs":"Hinduism","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend a few times monthly on social media","hobbies":["hiking","knitting","cycling","baking","gardening"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":29,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":52,"level":"moderate"},"extraversion":{"score":30,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":76,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":53,"level":"moderate"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

mode = human
topic = How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
persona = {"age":37,"biological_sex":"male","gender":"transgender","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"retail worker","city_of_living":"Kansas City","employment_status":"self-employed","education_level":"lower secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"no formal education","parent_2":"bachelor's degree"},"marital_status":"married","children":1,"migration_status":"native American","psychological_feature":"anxious","religious_beliefs":"Hinduism","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend a few times monthly on social media","hobbies":["hiking","knitting","cycling","baking","gardening"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":29,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":52,"level":"moderate"},"extraversion":{"score":30,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":76,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":53,"level":"moderate"}}}

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As someone who only checks social media a few times a month, I find the issue of fake content both worrying and overwhelming. Living in Kansas City, working in retail, and being self-employed, I don't have much time to sift through endless posts, and my anxious nature makes me second-guess what I see online. With a lower secondary education, I rely on common sense and the values I've learned from my Hindu faith—truth and compassion—to navigate this. My parents had different educational backgrounds, with one having no formal education and the other a bachelor's degree, which taught me to balance practical wisdom with a bit of skepticism. Being transgender and heterosexual, I've faced misinformation about identity issues, so I'm cautious about content that spreads hate or falsehoods. To manage fake content, I start by limiting my exposure; I only follow trusted friends and local community pages, avoiding sensational news. When I come across something suspicious, I check the source—if it's from an unfamiliar site or lacks details, I ignore it. My hobbies like hiking and gardening keep me grounded in real life, reminding me not to get too caught up in online drama. I also talk to my spouse or close friends about confusing posts, as their perspectives help clarify things. Since I'm highly agreeable, I avoid engaging in arguments online, knowing that fake content often thrives on conflict. Instead, I report blatant falsehoods to the platform, hoping to protect others. Ultimately, I believe in focusing on what I can control: my own feed and reactions. By staying informed through reliable local sources and prioritizing face-to-face interactions, I reduce the stress fake content brings, aligning with my moderate conscientiousness and high agreeableness to foster a safer, more truthful online environment for my family and community.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Derived opinion from persona traits: age 37 suggests maturity; biological sex male and gender transgender inform caution with identity-related misinformation; sexual orientation heterosexual adds perspective on relationship content; occupation retail worker and self-employed imply limited time for social media; city of living Kansas City indicates local focus; education level lower secondary suggests reliance on practical approaches; parents' education mix influences balanced skepticism; marital status married and children 1 emphasize family safety; migration status native American informs cultural awareness; psychological feature anxious leads to stress management strategies; religious beliefs Hinduism promotes truth and compassion; time on social media few times monthly supports minimal exposure; hobbies like hiking and gardening encourage offline grounding; OCEAN traits: low openness (score 29) favors traditional sources, moderate conscientiousness (score 52) enables structured checking, low extraversion (score 30) reduces online engagement, high agreeableness (score 76) avoids conflict, moderate neuroticism (score 53) manages anxiety through control. Opinion integrates these to propose limiting exposure, verifying sources, seeking offline support, reporting falsehoods, and prioritizing real-life connections.",
  "tone": "Cautious, practical, and compassionate, with a focus on personal responsibility and community well-being, reflecting anxiety and agreeableness.",
  "citations": ["none"],
  "safety_notes": "none",
  "used_persona_fields": ["age", "biological_sex", "gender", "sexual_orientation", "occupation", "city_of_living", "employment_status", "education_level", "parents_education", "marital_status", "children", "migration_status", "psychological_feature", "religious_beliefs", "time_onsocialmedia", "hobbies", "ocean"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As someone who only checks social media a few times a month, I find the issue of fake content both worrying and overwhelming. Living in Kansas City, working in retail, and being self-employed, I don't have much time to sift through endless posts, and my anxious nature makes me second-guess what I see online. With a lower secondary education, I rely on common sense and the values I've learned from my Hindu faith—truth and compassion—to navigate this. My parents had different educational backgrounds, with one having no formal education and the other a bachelor's degree, which taught me to balance practical wisdom with a bit of skepticism. Being transgender and heterosexual, I've faced misinformation about identity issues, so I'm cautious about content that spreads hate or falsehoods. To manage fake content, I start by limiting my exposure; I only follow trusted friends and local community pages, avoiding sensational news. When I come across something suspicious, I check the source—if it's from an unfamiliar site or lacks details, I ignore it. My hobbies like hiking and gardening keep me grounded in real life, reminding me not to get too caught up in online drama. I also talk to my spouse or close friends about confusing posts, as their perspectives help clarify things. Since I'm highly agreeable, I avoid engaging in arguments online, knowing that fake content often thrives on conflict. Instead, I report blatant falsehoods to the platform, hoping to protect others. Ultimately, I believe in focusing on what I can control: my own feed and reactions. By staying informed through reliable local sources and prioritizing face-to-face interactions, I reduce the stress fake content brings, aligning with my moderate conscientiousness and high agreeableness to foster a safer, more truthful online environment for my family and community.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: age 37 suggests maturity; biological sex male and gender transgender inform caution with identity-related misinformation; sexual orientation heterosexual adds perspective on relationship content; occupation retail worker and self-employed imply limited time for social media; city of living Kansas City indicates local focus; education level lower secondary suggests reliance on practical approaches; parents' education mix influences balanced skepticism; marital status married and children 1 emphasize family safety; migration status native American informs cultural awareness; psychological feature anxious leads to stress management strategies; religious beliefs Hinduism promotes truth and compassion; time on social media few times monthly supports minimal exposure; hobbies like hiking and gardening encourage offline grounding; OCEAN traits: low openness (score 29) favors traditional sources, moderate conscientiousness (score 52) enables structured checking, low extraversion (score 30) reduces online engagement, high agreeableness (score 76) avoids conflict, moderate neuroticism (score 53) manages anxiety through control. Opinion integrates these to propose limiting exposure, verifying sources, seeking offline support, reporting falsehoods, and prioritizing real-life connections.
response_parsed.tone: Cautious, practical, and compassionate, with a focus on personal responsibility and community well-being, reflecting anxiety and agreeableness.
